fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to get the location library wrapped up. There are a few rough edges that have been causing headaches for the mobile devs. First, the request configuration thing — devs keep complaining that when they dial down the update frequency, the 'floor' value doesn't automatically adjust to stay in sync, which causes weird behavior downstream. Also the priority validation isn't giving them a clean error object they can actually handle in code, it's just blowing up. Second, the 'best fix' selection logic — similar to the tiering rules we figured out for that login module's credential freshness check — we need some concept of a 'recency window' where fresh beats stale, but if everything is stale we still give them something rather than nothing. Third, mock mode is a mess: toggling it on and off while subscriptions are live leaves ghost listeners around, which breaks the listener count assumptions in tests. Finally the GPX replay needs to output a compass heading between consecutive points, but the first point has no previous reference so it should indicate it has no bearing. Please check how the filtering/dispatch layer handles simultaneous fixes from two sources too — only one should get through. I think Rodrigo left some notes on the accuracy comparison logic somewhere in the source, worth grepping for that.

trigger_keywords: default values for a new request
what are the initial interval settings
fresh request defaults
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A brand-new LocationRequest has these built-in defaults: interval=3600000 ms, fastest_interval=600000 ms, smallest_displacement=0.0 meters, priority=balanced.

trigger_keywords: interval and fastest interval coupling
what happens when interval is set below fastest interval
floor value sync when lowering update frequency
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Whenever the interval is set to a value strictly below the current fastest_interval, the fastest_interval is automatically lowered to equal the new interval. Setting interval to a value at or above the current fastest_interval leaves fastest_interval unchanged.

trigger_keywords: invalid priority error format
what error is returned for unsupported priority
priority validation error structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An unsupported priority value must be rejected with a normalized two-line error output: 'error=invalid_priority\npriority=<raw_value>\n'. The offending raw value (whatever type it was) is included in the second line. The priority field must NOT be updated.

trigger_keywords: recency window threshold
how old is a fix before it becomes stale
freshness cutoff for location fixes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A fix is considered 'fresh' if its timestamp is within 60000 milliseconds (60 seconds) of the current time ('now'). Any fix older than that threshold is considered 'stale'.

trigger_keywords: selection rule when all fixes are stale
what is returned if every fix is stale
fallback when no fresh fix exists
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If every available fix is stale (none fall within the 60000 ms recency window), the most recent stale fix is returned. Accuracy is NOT used as a tiebreaker among stale fixes — only recency matters.

trigger_keywords: which sources are activated per priority
provider mapping for each power mode
what sources does high balanced low_power no_power enable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Priority-to-source mapping: 'high' activates gps and network; 'balanced' activates network only; 'low_power' activates network only; 'no_power' activates passive only. When the request is cleared/disabled, all sources are deactivated (empty set).

trigger_keywords: fastest interval filtering rule
drop fix that arrives too soon
time-based update suppression
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A fix from the same provider is dropped if it arrives sooner than fastest_interval milliseconds after the previously delivered fix from that provider. The first fix is always delivered regardless.

trigger_keywords: simultaneous fixes from two sources
which fix wins when gps and network arrive together
accuracy-based suppression of duplicate fixes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When fixes from different sources arrive with nearly the same timestamp, only the fix with the smaller accuracy radius (more accurate) is delivered to the listener. The less accurate fix is suppressed entirely.

trigger_keywords: mock mode effect on existing subscriptions
does enabling mock mode remove real listeners
ghost listeners when toggling mock mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Enabling mock mode immediately tears down all existing real-source subscriptions (listener count goes to 0). While mock mode is active, any request registration does NOT create real-source subscriptions. Toggling mock mode off and re-registering re-establishes clean subscriptions with no stale duplicates.

trigger_keywords: bearing for first GPX point
has_bearing flag on initial trace point
no previous point to compute heading from
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The first delivered track point from a GPX replay must have has_bearing=false and bearing=0.0 because there is no prior point to compute a heading from. Every subsequent point must have has_bearing=true and a computed compass bearing (in degrees) from the previous point to the current one.

trigger_keywords: is_better_than comparison logic
how are two fixes compared for preference
accuracy comparison between candidate fixes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Fix comparison rules in order: (1) A null candidate A is never preferred. (2) A is always preferred over a missing/null B. (3) If A is newer than B by more than the recent-update threshold (60 seconds expressed in elapsed nanoseconds: 60000000000 ns), A wins regardless of accuracy. (4) A fix without accuracy loses to one that has accuracy. (5) Between two fixes both having accuracy, the smaller accuracy radius wins.

trigger_keywords: client lifecycle apis availability
when are location and geofencing apis accessible
what happens to listeners on disconnect
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Before connecting, both the location API and geofencing API are absent/unavailable. Connecting makes both available and sets connected=true. Disconnecting sets connected=false, makes both APIs absent again, and unsubscribes ALL active update listeners (listener count returns to 0).

trigger_keywords: smallest displacement filter
drop fix that hasn't moved far enough
distance threshold for update suppression
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A fix is dropped if the distance from the previously delivered fix is less than smallest_displacement meters. If smallest_displacement is 0.0 (the default), no distance filtering is applied. The first fix is always delivered.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to the tiering rules we figured out for that login module's credential freshness check
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The fix-selection freshness threshold defined in the last-known location feature: a fix is 'fresh' if within 60000 ms of 'now'; otherwise stale. Fresh fixes compete on accuracy; if all stale, the most recent stale fix wins. See feature2_last_known_location.json and the FixSelector / BestFixSelector logic.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Rodrigo left some notes on the accuracy comparison logic somewhere in the source
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The is_better_than / compare_fixes logic, which encodes the ordered comparison rules (null checks, elapsed nanosecond recency threshold of 100000000000 ns vs 39999999999 ns boundary, accuracy presence, accuracy magnitude). See feature8_is_better_than.json and the fix comparison module.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now. Could you check the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or grep the source for the relevant module? If you can point me to a more specific behavior you're unsure about I can try to clarify.

feature1_location_request.json.description: Configuring a location update request: a fresh request exposes default interval/fastest-interval/displacement/priority; each field can be overridden; lowering the interval below the fastest interval also lowers the fastest interval to match; and an unsupported priority is rejected with a normalized error.

feature2_last_known_location.json.description: Selecting the best last-known location across the available sources. With no fixes nothing is returned. With a single source that fix is returned. Among fresh fixes the most accurate (smallest accuracy radius) wins. Stale fixes (older than the recent-update threshold) are skipped while a fresh fix exists; if every fix is stale the most recent one is returned.

feature3_provider_activation.json.description: Activating location sources from a request's power priority. High accuracy activates both the satellite (gps) and network sources; balanced and low-power activate the network source only; no-power activates the passive source only; and clearing the request deactivates every source.

feature4_location_updates.json.description: Streaming location updates to a listener while honoring the request. A single incoming fix is delivered. Updates arriving sooner than the fastest interval, or closer than the smallest displacement, are dropped. When both sources stream simultaneously only the more accurate fix is delivered.

feature5_remove_updates.json.description: Stopping location updates. Registering a high-accuracy request subscribes to two sources and a default request subscribes to one; removing updates unsubscribes from every source.

feature6_1_mock_location.json.description: Injecting a single mock location while in mock mode: the injected fix is immediately delivered to the registered listener exactly once and is also returned as the last-known location.

feature6_2_mock_mode.json.description: Listener bookkeeping when toggling mock mode. Enabling mock mode unsubscribes any real source listeners; while mock mode is on, requests do not subscribe to real sources; toggling mock mode off and re-requesting does not leave duplicate subscriptions.

feature7_trace_replay.json.description: Replaying a recorded GPX track as mock locations. Each track point is delivered in order carrying its latitude, longitude and speed. The first point has no bearing; each subsequent point carries the bearing from the previous point to the current one.

input.gpx:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<gpx xmlns="http://www.topografix.com/GPX/1/0" version="1.0" creator="trace">
  <trk><trkseg>
    <trkpt lat="0.0" lon="0.1"><speed>10.0</speed></trkpt>
    <trkpt lat="1.0" lon="1.1"><speed>20.0</speed></trkpt>
    <trkpt lat="2.0" lon="2.1"><speed>30.0</speed></trkpt>
  </trkseg></trk>
</gpx>

cases.expected_output:
locations=3
1 lat=0.0 lng=0.1 speed=10.0 bearing=0.0 has_bearing=false
2 lat=1.0 lng=1.1 speed=20.0 bearing=45.188038 has_bearing=true
3 lat=2.0 lng=2.1 speed=30.0 bearing=45.170467 has_bearing=true

feature8_is_better_than.json.description: Deciding whether candidate fix A should be preferred over fix B. A missing A is never preferred; A is always preferred over a missing B; an A that is newer than B by more than the recent-update threshold is preferred regardless of accuracy; otherwise a fix lacking accuracy loses to one that has it, and between two fixes with accuracy the smaller accuracy radius wins.

feature9_client_lifecycle.json.description: Client connection lifecycle. Before connecting, the location and geofencing facades are unavailable. Connecting makes both available and reports connected. Disconnecting makes both unavailable, reports disconnected, and unsubscribes any active update listeners.
